title: 41 — After
content: The lecture hall smells like dry-erase markers and old radiators. Autumn presses against the tall windows, leaves going copper along the edges, and I am writing in a notebook — actual paper, actual ink — and my hands are steady.
Steady.

I notice this now. I notice my hands the way you notice a door that used to stick suddenly opening smooth. The absence of the resistance is the thing. My pen moves across the page and I am taking notes on cognitive behavioral therapy, on schema models, on the architecture of belief, and Dr. Amara is talking about gaslighting.

"—the therapeutic context for understanding gaslighting must move beyond the interpersonal," she says. She paces the front of the hall with her sleeves rolled to her elbows. "The most insidious gaslighting environments are institutional. They don't require a single malicious actor. They require a system that benefits from the subject's uncertainty."
I write this down. I underline *system*. I underline *uncertainty*. My handwriting is small and precise and it does not shake.

I am twenty-three years old. I am a first-year doctoral student at University College London. I am sitting in a lecture hall with sixty other people and none of them know what I know about gaslighting, about institutions, about what a system can do to a mind that was already inclined — by temperament, by trauma, by the simple accident of being young and alone — to doubt itself.
But they will. Eventually. That's what the degree is for.
I live in a flat.

I want to say this plainly because it matters more than anything else in this chapter. I live in a flat in Kentish Town. It is small. The furniture came from a charity shop on Fortress Road — a sofa with a slight concavity in the middle where someone else's years have pressed into it, a kitchen table with a water ring I covered with a potted plant, a desk that wobbles unless I fold a bit of cardboard under the left leg. The cardboard is from a cereal box. Crunchy Nut Corn Flakes. I chose them for the cartoon bee on the front. It seemed like the kind of thing a person would choose.

The window overlooks a park. Not a grand park. A patch of green with a few benches and a bin and dog walkers in rain jackets. In the morning I can hear the dogs bark. In the evening I can hear the gate latch when the council locks it. At night there's just the sound of my own breathing and the occasional siren threading down the road like a long silver needle.
I don't stream.

I need to say that again. I don't stream. I don't post. I deleted my Reddit account, my TikTok, my Instagram. The usernames are gone. The followers are gone. The comments — all those comments, thousands of them, each one a small verdict delivered by a stranger in a bedroom somewhere — are gone. I didn't archive them. I didn't screenshot them. I burned them down the way you burn a house you no longer need to live in.
My therapist — Linda, who has a face like a friendly building and an office that smells of lavender and old carpet — asked me if that felt like erasing myself.
"No," I told her. "It felt like stopping other people from writing on me."
She nodded. She does that a lot. She nods as if what I've said is obvious and true and she's just pleased I've caught up to it.

I go running in the park. Three times a week. I am bad at it. My form is terrible and I breathe like a broken radiator and my face goes the color of a brick. But I go. I go because my body is mine and I can move it through space and the ground is solid beneath my shoes and there is no audience. Nobody is watching. Nobody is counting my steps or reading my heart rate or turning my breath into a metric. It's just me and the path and the dogs and the grey London sky.

I cook badly. I want to be honest about this. Last week I made pasta and forgot to salt the water and the sauce was from a jar and I burned the garlic bread. I stood in my tiny kitchen with the smoke alarm screaming and the window open and the autumn air coming in, and I laughed. I laughed because I had burned garlic bread in a flat that was mine, in a life that was mine, and nobody was here to tell me I was doing it wrong or right or for the cameras. The smoke alarm was just a smoke alarm. The burned bread was just burned bread.

This is what I'm learning: ordinariness is harder than being extraordinary. Being a phenomenon is easy — the world does the work for you. It slots you into its machinery and turns the crank and you go where it pushes. Being nobody, being just a person who buys Crunchy Nut Corn Flakes and burns garlic bread and runs badly three times a week — that requires invention. That requires making yourself from scratch.
It's harder.
It's better.
Helen Webb wrote a book.
*Nine Years: A Memoir of Institutional Abuse.*

It's a bestseller, which is a word I don't think either of us ever expected to attach to anything in our lives. She's on tour. She passes through London on a Thursday and texts me — just a text, no ceremony, no negotiation through intermediaries — and we meet for coffee.

The café is on Marchmont Street. It has wooden tables and a chalkboard menu and steam on the windows because it's cold outside and warm inside and that's what happens. Helen is already there when I arrive, sitting in the corner with her coat draped over the chair back and a mug between her hands, and she looks up and she smiles.
And I realize — standing in the doorway with the cold at my back — that I have never seen Helen Webb look relaxed.

Not once. Not in all the months at Bly, not in the solicitors' offices, not in the trial's aftermath. She was always performing, even when she thought she wasn't. Compliance was her first language. She'd learned it so early and so thoroughly that not-performing felt like not-breathing. Every movement was a calculation. Every silence was a strategy.
Now she sits in a café with steam on the windows and she is just a woman having coffee with a friend.
"Maeve." She says my name like it's a greeting and an apology and a relief, all in one syllable.

I sit across from her. The chair scrapes. The table is small enough that our mugs are almost touching. The coffee steam rises between us and I can smell it — dark and bitter and real, not a metaphor, not a symbol, just coffee in a café on a Thursday in London.
"You look well," I say.

"I feel well," she says. Then she laughs. It's a short, surprised sound, like she didn't expect the words to be true. "That's strange to say out loud. I keep waiting for someone to tell me I'm not allowed."
"No one's going to tell you that."
"No." She turns her mug between her hands. "No, I suppose not."

We talk about the tour. About the interviews, the audiences, the women who come up to her after readings with their own stories pressed between their teeth like something they've been waiting years to say aloud. We talk about the publisher, who is kind but keeps suggesting she do a podcast, and she says the word *podcast* the way you'd say *spider*. We talk about nothing, in the way that friends talk about nothing — the weather, the coffee, the bus strike, the way London in autumn always looks like it's about to rain and then doesn't or then does.
And then she says it.
"I still do jigsaw puzzles. Can't help it." She looks almost embarrassed. "But now I do them for fun, not for survival."

I hold my mug and the ceramic is warm against my palms and the steam touches my face and I think: this is grace. This moment. This woman who spent nine years assembling small pieces into a controlled image because control was the only thing between her and annihilation, now doing it because the pieces are pretty and the picture is pleasant and she can stop whenever she wants.
"That's—" I start. "That's good, Helen."
"It is," she says. "It really is."

We sit for another hour. Maybe longer. The café empties and fills and empties again. The light outside changes from grey to gold to the early dark of a London evening. When we leave, she hugs me on the street. It's brief and firm and a little awkward because Helen is not a person who hugs — or she wasn't, before. She is learning to be a person who hugs. She is learning to be a person, full stop.
"Take care of yourself, Maeve."
"You too."
She walks toward the tube and I walk toward home and the air is cold and sharp and tastes like the beginning of winter and I am alive and so is she and that is enough. That is more than enough.
Flora writes me letters.

Real letters. On paper. In an envelope with a stamp. She's at a boarding school in Hampshire — her choice, her request, emphatic and specific. She wanted to be around people her own age. She wanted walls that didn't have histories. She wanted a room with a lock on the inside.

The letters come once a week, sometimes twice. Her handwriting is round and earnest and she dotsher i's with little circles, which I pretend to find childish but actually find dear. She tells me about lessons (she likes biology, dislikes French), about the other girls (they are complicated and she is learning that friendship is not either total devotion or total absence — there is a middle, she says, and it's messy but good), about the food (she describes the food in lavish, excruciating detail, because she knows I cook badly and she thinks this is funny).

The latest letter arrives on a Saturday. I open it at the kitchen table with the water ring and the potted plant and the wobbly leg. The paper is blue. She likes blue paper. I don't know why and I haven't asked.
*Dear Maeve,*
*I joined the drama club. I'm playing a ghost. It's very funny. Love, Flora.*
I read it three times.

She's playing a ghost. Flora Ashford, who spent her childhood in a house that was itself a performance of haunting, is on a stage in Hampshire pretending to be dead. She's fourteen. She's in a school play. She has lines to memorize and a costume to wear and she'll stand in the wings with other girls her age and they'll whisper and giggle and be anxious about forgetting their cues and after the show there will be flowers and punch and parents in the audience clapping too loudly.

That's health. That's what health looks like for a girl who was raised inside a narrative someone else wrote. She takes the thing that was done to her and she puts it on a stage where it belongs. A ghost in fiction. Not in life.

I pin the letter to the corkboard above my desk, next to the other letters, next to the photo of the park and the recipe I printed out for chickpea stew that I'll probably burn. The blue paper brightens the wall.
Playing a ghost. It's very funny.
It is.
Do I still see them?
Sometimes.

In the corner of a mirror — a flash of something moving that isn't there when I turn. In the angle of a shadow after the lamp clicks off. In the half-second between waking and knowing where I am, when the ceiling could be any ceiling and the dark could be any dark and I am seventeen again, or fifteen, or twelve, standing in a hallway that goes on too long.
But I know what they are now.

They are not spirits. They are not Miss Jessel come back from the lake. They are not Peter Quint standing at the window. They are not the Bly Manor ghost, the internet phantom, the thing the comment threads named and claimed and turned into content.

They are memories. My mind processing what happened. Firing old patterns, tracing old neural routes, doing what brains do when they've been shaped by specific and sustained terror. The ghosts are my brain doing its job — alerting me to patterns that once meant danger, checking whether the threat is still present, filing the sensory data in the cabinets it built for storage.
I let them pass. I don't chase them. I don't narrate them. I don't build a story around them and I don't post the story and I don't read the comments about the story.

The ghosts are just ghosts. And ghosts, I've learned, are not supernatural. They're cognitive. They're what happens when the past insists on being felt in the present. My mind has earned the right to show me its scars. I respect that.
I blink. The mirror shows my face. The shadow resolves into a coat on a hook. The dark gives way to morning.
I make coffee. I sit at the wobbly desk. I work.
The paper is about recovered memory and suggestibility.

It's my first solo submission. Thirty-two pages, APA format, eighty-one references. I've been writing it for weeks, carving the argument out of the literature and my own experience — though I don't cite my experience, because this is science and science requires the illusion of distance even when there is none.

I'm on the last section. The conclusion. I'm writing about gaslighting — the institutional kind, the kind that doesn't require a single villain, the kind that grows like moss in the cracks of power structures where no one person is responsible and everyone is complicit. I'm writing about the narratives that victims construct. How they're not lies, exactly, but survival strategies. How the brain builds a story to get through what it cannot endure, and then the story becomes the structure and the structure becomes the identity and the identity becomes the thing you can't afford to question because questioning it means questioning everything.
I stop.
I read what I've written:
*The most dangerous form of gaslighting is the kind the victim performs on herself — the narrative she constructs to survive, which then becomes the lie she must maintain to exist.*
I stare at the sentence.

It's about Peter's governesses. The women who came before me, who entered that house and were reshaped by it, who told themselves stories about their purpose there until the stories became the only thing keeping them upright. Jessel, most of all, but others too — I've read the records now, the employment histories, the quiet departures, the references not given. Each of them built a narrative. Each of them believed it.

It's about Helen. Nine years of performing compliance until the performance became the person and the person forgot there was ever a role. "I still do jigsaw puzzles." Of course she does. The pattern of finding order in fragments is so deeply embedded in her neural architecture that it would be more traumatic to stop than to continue. She does them for fun now. The structure remains. The meaning has changed.
And it's about me.

I know this. I've known it for a while, in the way you know things you're not ready to say out loud. The narrative I constructed at Bly — the haunting, the mystery, the significance — was how I survived. I needed to believe the house was extraordinary because if the house was extraordinary then I was extraordinary for being in it, and if I was extraordinary then I mattered, and if I mattered then the things that were done to me mattered too. The story wasn't a lie. It was a life raft. But a life raft, if you hold onto it too long, becomes a cage.

I wrote myself into a ghost story because ghost stories have ghosts and ghosts have meaning and meaning is the thing that makes suffering bearable. The alternative — that suffering can be senseless, that cruelty can be banal, that a man like Peter Ashford can damage people not because he's a monster but because he's ordinary and powerful and indifferent — the alternative is harder to live with. So I told myself a story. I performed it. I posted it. I watched other people perform it back to me, add their own verses, their own theories, their own hunger for meaning.
The lie I maintained to exist.

I look at the sentence on the screen. The cursor blinks beside the period. The apartment is quiet except for the sound of the park and the distant bus and the fridge humming its small mechanical song.
Do I delete it?
No.

It's the truth. Not the whole truth — no single sentence can hold that — but it's a true one. And the difference between me-now and me-then is that me-now can write a true sentence about her own deception and leave it on the page. Not spin it. Not deflect it. Not perform surprise or shame or redemption around it. Just: this is true. This happened to me, and I did it to myself, and I know, and I'm telling you anyway.
That's what awareness is. Not the absence of unreliability. The acknowledgment of it.
I leave the sentence.

Two months later, the paper is published in the *Journal of Trauma and Dissociation*. My first academic publication. The journal arrives in the post — because I asked for a physical copy, because I wanted to hold it — and I stand in my kitchen with the wobbly table and the burned garlic bread still faintly scenting the air, and I look at my name on the cover.
*Maeve Bly, MSc.*

Not Peter's daughter. Not Caroline's daughter. Not the Bly Manor girl, not the internet phenomenon, not the subject of a thousandReddit threads and a twice-viewed livestream and a trial that made the papers for all the wrong reasons.
Just Maeve.
Finally.

I put the journal on the shelf next to Flora's letters and Helen's book and the photo of the park and the recipe for chickpea stew I still haven't made properly. I go to the window. The park is there, grey and green and ordinary. A dog barks. A gate closes. The light is doing that thing London light does in winter where it can't decide whether to commit to gold or give up entirely.
I breathe.

The air comes in cold and goes out warm. My lungs work. My heart works. My mind works — unreliably, yes, with bias and pattern-matching and old furniture in the corners that I keep bumping into. But it works. It knows what it is. It knows what I am.

I am Maeve Bly. I live in a flat with cheap furniture. I run badly. I cook worse. I study the architecture of belief because I've lived inside one and I know how the walls go up and where the doors are hidden and what happens when you stop pretending the house is haunted and start admitting it was just a house where bad things happened to ordinary people.
I am ordinary.

That's the hardest word I've ever learned to say about myself. Harder than *abuse*. Harder than *gaslighting*. Harder than *survivor*. Because *survivor* has drama in it, and drama is just another story, and I am done with stories.

I am ordinary, and I am alive, and the coffee I'm making right now is probably going to be too strong because I always use too much and I never learn, and that's not a metaphor — I just genuinely use too much coffee. Every time. I know I do. I do it anyway.
The water boils.
I pour.
The steam rises, warm against my face, and I drink my bad coffee in my small flat and I don't look for ghosts.
Not because they aren't there.
Because I know what they are now.
And they can wait.
*End of Book*
